section.title: The Flight to Egypt
section.sectionRationale: The joy of the Magi gives way to danger. Warned in a dream, Joseph flees with the Child to Egypt; Herod, enraged, slaughters the infants of Bethlehem; and after Herod's death the family returns and settles in Nazareth. Matthew threads three fulfilled prophecies through the account, presenting Jesus as the true Israel and the true Son called out of Egypt.
section.thesis: In flight, in slaughter, and in return, the infant Messiah relives and redeems the story of Israel, called out of Egypt as God's Son, surrounded by weeping yet destined to save, so that from His earliest days the shadow of the cross and the sovereignty of God lie over His life.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the escape to Egypt; the massacre at Bethlehem and Rachel's weeping; the death of Herod and the return; and the settling in Nazareth.

verses.text: When the Magi had gone, an angel of the Lord appeared to Joseph in a {{onar|dream}}. “Get up!” he said. “Take the Child and His mother and flee to Egypt. Stay there until I tell you, for Herod is going to search for the Child to kill Him.”
So he got up, took the Child and His mother by night, and withdrew to Egypt,
where he stayed until the death of Herod. This {{pleroo|fulfilled}} what the Lord had spoken through the prophet: “Out of Egypt I called My {{huios|Son}}.”

When Herod saw that he had been outwitted by the Magi, he was filled with rage. Sending orders, he put to death all the boys in Bethlehem and its vicinity who were two years old and under, according to the time he had learned from the Magi.
Then what was spoken through the prophet Jeremiah was fulfilled:
“A voice is heard in Ramah, weeping and great mourning, Rachel weeping for her children and refusing to be comforted, because they are no more.”

units.label: The return from Egypt
units.note: those seeking the Child's life are dead

verses.text: After Herod died, an angel of the Lord appeared in a dream to Joseph in Egypt.
“Get up!” he said. “Take the Child and His mother and go to the land of Israel, for those seeking the Child’s life are now dead.”
So Joseph got up, took the Child and His mother, and went to the land of Israel.
But when he learned that Archelaus was reigning in Judea in place of his father Herod, he was afraid to go there. And having been warned in a dream, he withdrew to the district of Galilee,
and he went and lived in a town called Nazareth. So was fulfilled what was spoken through the prophets: “He will be called a {{nazoraios|Nazarene}}.”

onar.gloss: A dream. Matthew's infancy narrative is guided by dreams, the way God directed Joseph in Genesis; here God warns and steers His people through the night, protecting the Child at every turn.

pleroo.gloss: To fill up, complete, fulfill. Matthew's signature word for showing that Jesus brings the Scriptures to their intended goal. Three times in this chapter events fulfill prophecy, presenting Jesus as the one in whom Israel's story reaches its fullness.

huios.gloss: Son. Hosea's out of Egypt I called My Son first named Israel, God's son; Matthew applies it to Jesus, the true Son and true Israel, who relives His people's story and succeeds where they failed.

nazoraios.gloss: Nazarene, a person from Nazareth, an obscure and lightly regarded town. The title likely gathers the prophetic theme of a despised, lowly Messiah, and may echo the branch, netzer, of Isaiah 11:1, the shoot from Jesse's stump.

basic.context: Warned by an angel in a dream, Joseph takes Mary and Jesus and flees by night to Egypt, escaping Herod's reach. Enraged at being outwitted, Herod murders all the baby boys around Bethlehem. After Herod dies, an angel calls the family back, but fearing Herod's son Archelaus in Judea, Joseph settles in Nazareth in the north. Matthew marks each move with a fulfilled Scripture.
basic.hermeneutics: Matthew reads the events through the lens of the Old Testament, showing Jesus reliving Israel's history. Out of Egypt was first said of the nation; Matthew applies it to Jesus, the true Son. Rachel's weeping, first about the exile, now sounds over Bethlehem. Read these quotations as patterns fulfilled, not merely predictions ticked off.
basic.originalLanguages: God guides by a dream, as He guided the patriarch Joseph long before. Matthew's key word is fulfill, used three times here: Jesus brings the Scriptures to their goal. Out of Egypt I called My Son names Jesus as the true Son and true Israel. And Nazarene likely marks Him as the despised, lowly one the prophets foresaw.
basic.typology: Jesus recapitulates Israel's story: like the nation, He goes down to Egypt and is called out again. Like Moses, He survives a king's slaughter of infants and will one day lead a greater exodus. The weeping of Rachel, tied in Jeremiah to the promise of return, frames even this sorrow with hope.
basic.christ: Jesus is the true Son of God and the true Israel, called out of Egypt to succeed where the nation failed. From His infancy He is hunted, and innocents die around Him, foreshadowing the cross. He is the lowly Nazarene, despised by men, in whom the prophetic hope of a humble, suffering Messiah is fulfilled.
basic.god: God protects His Son through dreams and warnings, overruling the murderous power of kings. He is sovereign even over Herod's evil, weaving it into a story He has long foretold. Yet He does not shield the world from all grief; the tears of Bethlehem are real, and God enters that grief in His Son.
basic.crossReferences: Hosea 11:1 is quoted, out of Egypt I called My Son. Jeremiah 31:15 gives Rachel's weeping, set within a chapter of comfort and return. Exodus 1 and 2 tell of Pharaoh's slaughter and Moses' rescue, the pattern behind Herod. And Isaiah 11:1 speaks of the branch from Jesse.
basic.oneStory: The Messiah's first days retrace and redeem the story of His people. He goes down into Egypt and comes out again as the true Son; He escapes a tyrant's sword as Moses did; He settles in lowly Nazareth as the despised one the prophets foresaw. Suffering shadows Him from the cradle, but so does the sovereign, saving purpose of God.

context.literary: The account is organized around three fulfillment quotations, Hosea, Jeremiah, and a summary from the prophets, each anchored to a place: Egypt, Ramah, Nazareth. The repeated pattern of angelic dream, obedient action, and fulfilled Scripture binds the chapter and casts Jesus as the new Israel and the new Moses.
context.historical: Egypt lay outside Herod's jurisdiction and had long been a refuge for Jews, with sizable Jewish communities. Herod's slaughter of the infants fits what history records of his cruelty near the end of his reign. Archelaus, who succeeded him in Judea, was so brutal that Rome deposed him, which explains Joseph's fear and the move to Galilee.
context.canonical: The three citations gather Israel's story into Jesus: the exodus (Hosea 11:1), the exile and its promised comfort (Jeremiah 31:15), and the prophetic theme of the lowly, despised Messiah (the branch of Isaiah 11:1 and related texts). Matthew thereby presents Jesus as the true Israel, the new Moses, and the humble King foretold.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God sovereignly protecting the Messiah and fulfilling Scripture through the flight, the massacre, and the return, presenting Jesus as the true Son called out of Egypt, the new Moses, and the lowly Nazarene the prophets foretold.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The events are described as unique history, not as a template for guidance by dreams or for our own decisions. Joseph's prompt, trusting obedience is worth imitating. The abiding truths are God's sovereignty over evil, His faithfulness to His word, and Jesus' identity as the true Son.

debates.question: How can Hosea 11:1, which speaks of Israel's exodus, be fulfilled in Jesus?

views.label: Typological fulfillment: Jesus is the true Israel
views.case: Hosea recalls the past exodus of God's son, the nation; Matthew sees the pattern completed in Jesus, the true and faithful Son who relives Israel's journey and succeeds where the nation failed.

views.label: A fuller meaning intended by God
views.case: The Spirit who inspired Hosea embedded a deeper significance realized only in Christ, so that the historical statement about Israel finds its ultimate reference in God's greater Son.

debates.bearing: Both affirm that Matthew reads Scripture rightly, not by twisting Hosea but by seeing Jesus as the goal toward which Israel's whole story pointed.

debates.question: Where is the prophecy that He will be called a Nazarene?

views.label: A theme drawn from the prophets, not one verse
views.case: Matthew says prophets, plural, and cites no single text; Nazarene evokes the lowly, despised Messiah the prophets describe, an obscure origin fitting the rejected servant.

views.label: A wordplay on the branch, netzer, of Isaiah 11:1
views.case: Nazareth may echo the Hebrew netzer, the shoot from Jesse's stump; calling Jesus a Nazarene quietly names Him the promised Branch of David's line.

debates.bearing: Either way the point holds: the Messiah comes from a place of no reputation, fulfilling the prophetic portrait of one despised yet chosen.

study.originalLanguages: God directs events by an onar, a dream, guiding Joseph as He once guided the patriarch of the same name. Matthew's key term is pleroo, to fulfill, sounded three times: in Jesus the Scriptures reach their goal. Hosea's word calls Israel God's huios, His Son; Matthew applies it to Jesus, the true Son and true Israel, brought out of Egypt. And the child settles in Nazareth to be a Nazoraios, a Nazarene, a title of lowliness that likely gathers the prophetic theme of a despised Messiah and may echo the netzer, the branch, of Isaiah 11:1.

typology.type: Out of Egypt I called My Son
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the true Israel, relives the nation's descent into Egypt and exodus out of it, succeeding where Israel failed, Hosea 11:1.

typology.type: The new Moses spared from a king's slaughter
typology.fulfillment: As Moses survived Pharaoh's killing of the infants and led the exodus, Jesus survives Herod and leads a greater deliverance, Exodus 1-2.
Jeremiah's lament sits within a promise of return and hope; the sorrow of Bethlehem is taken up into the salvation Jesus brings, Jeremiah 31:15-17.

study.christ: From His first days Jesus is both the sovereignly protected Son and the hunted, sorrow-shadowed Savior. He is the true Israel, called out of Egypt, and the new Moses, spared from a tyrant's sword to lead a greater exodus from sin. The slaughter of the innocents throws the shadow of the cross across the manger: violence and grief gather around Him, and He will finally take that grief into Himself. He settles as a lowly Nazarene, a man of no reputation, in whom the prophets' portrait of a despised Messiah comes true, the Branch from Jesse's fallen line.
study.god: God is unmistakably sovereign here. He warns Joseph before Herod strikes, He appoints the refuge in Egypt, He announces the tyrant's death, and He redirects the family to Galilee, all in fulfillment of words spoken centuries earlier. Herod's rage cannot touch the purpose of God. Yet God does not spare the world every sorrow; the tears of Bethlehem are not explained away but held within a larger promise of comfort and return. The God who governs kings also enters human grief in His own Son.

crossReferences.note: Out of Egypt I called My Son, first of Israel, fulfilled in Jesus the true Son.
Rachel's weeping, set within a promise of return and hope for her children.
Pharaoh's slaughter of the infants and Moses' rescue, the pattern behind Herod and Jesus.
The branch, netzer, from the stump of Jesse, possibly echoed in the title Nazarene.

study.oneStory: The Messiah's infancy retraces the whole path of His people and begins to redeem it. He goes down to Egypt and is called out as the true Son; He escapes the sword as a new Moses; He grows up in despised Nazareth as the lowly one the prophets foretold. Around Him gather the sorrow of a weeping mother and the hostility of a murderous king, the very things that will meet Him at the cross. Yet over it all stands the sovereign hand of God, keeping His word and guarding His Savior for the day of salvation.

quiz.q: Why did Joseph take the Child and His mother to Egypt?

options.text: An angel warned him in a dream that Herod would seek to kill the Child.
options.note: Verse 13 gives the angel's warning as the reason for the flight.

options.text: He wanted to keep the family near the Jewish community abroad.
options.note: Egypt did have Jewish communities, but the stated reason is the angel's warning about Herod.

options.text: He was fleeing a famine in Judea.
options.note: There is no famine here; the danger was Herod's murderous intent.

options.text: He was sent by Herod to search elsewhere for the Child.
options.note: Joseph fled from Herod, warned by God, not on any errand of Herod's.

quiz.q: What does the quotation Out of Egypt I called My Son reveal about Jesus?

options.text: That Jesus is the true Israel and true Son, reliving and redeeming His people's story.
options.note: Matthew applies Hosea's word about Israel to Jesus, the greater Son.

options.text: That God gave the words of Hosea a fuller meaning realized in Christ.
options.note: True as a description of how the text is fulfilled, but it understates the point that Jesus is the true Israel.

options.text: That the flight to Egypt was an accident of history.
options.note: Matthew presents it as fulfillment of Scripture, not accident.

options.text: That Egypt would become the center of the kingdom.
options.note: Egypt is a temporary refuge, not the center of anything; the point is Jesus as the Son called out.

quiz.q: How does Matthew frame Herod's slaughter of the infants?

options.text: As fulfilling Jeremiah's word of Rachel weeping, a grief set within a promise of comfort.
options.note: Verses 17-18 cite Jeremiah 31:15, whose context holds out hope and return.

options.text: As proof that God had lost control of events.
options.note: The opposite; God is sovereign throughout, though real grief is not denied. This misreads the framing.

options.text: As a random act with no connection to Scripture.
options.note: Matthew ties it directly to Jeremiah's prophecy, not to randomness.

options.text: As a judgment God sent on Bethlehem for sin.
options.note: The massacre is Herod's evil, not a divine judgment on the town.

quiz.q: Why did Joseph settle in Nazareth rather than Judea?

options.text: He feared Archelaus, Herod's son, and was warned in a dream to go to Galilee.
options.note: Verse 22 gives both his fear of Archelaus and the dream warning.

options.text: He simply preferred the quiet of a small northern town.
options.note: Nazareth was obscure, but the text names fear of Archelaus and a divine warning as the reasons.

options.text: Nazareth was his and Mary's original hometown by birth.
options.note: They settle there, but the passage stresses the dream warning and the danger in Judea, not simply returning home.

options.text: The angel commanded him to build the kingdom's capital there.
options.note: No such command is given; the move is for safety and fulfillment of prophecy.

quiz.q: What does the title Nazarene signify about the Messiah?

options.text: That He comes from a place of no reputation, fulfilling the prophetic portrait of a lowly, despised Messiah.
options.note: Nazareth's obscurity fits the prophets' theme of a humble, rejected Savior, perhaps echoing the branch of Isaiah 11:1.

options.text: That He is the promised Branch, netzer, from Jesse's line.
options.note: A plausible wordplay, but it is one proposed sense; the broader point is His lowliness foretold by the prophets.

options.text: That He belonged to a strict religious sect at Nazareth.
options.note: Nazarene here means from Nazareth, not membership in a sect.

options.text: That He was to be a wealthy and celebrated ruler.
options.note: The opposite; the title marks His humble, despised origins.

questions: God guided and protected His Son through danger without removing the danger. How does this shape the way you trust God in seasons when He leads you through hardship rather than around it?
Herod's rage could not overturn God's purpose. Where do you need to rest in God's sovereignty over powers and events that feel threatening in your own life?
The tears of Bethlehem were real, and Scripture does not explain them away. How does it comfort you that God enters human grief in His Son rather than standing aloof from it?
Jesus grew up in lowly, unremarkable Nazareth. How does the humble origin of your Savior challenge the value you place on status and reputation?
Joseph obeyed each dream promptly, even at great cost and disruption. Where is God calling you to obey quickly and trust Him with the consequences?

translationNotes.note: The quotation is Hosea 11:1, which originally recalls Israel's exodus; Matthew applies it to Jesus as the true Son and true Israel.
The citation is Jeremiah 31:15. In Jeremiah the lament stands within a chapter of comfort and promised return, so the sorrow is framed by hope.

No single Old Testament verse reads He will be called a Nazarene; Matthew says prophets, plural, likely summarizing the theme of a despised, lowly Messiah, and possibly playing on netzer, branch, in Isaiah 11:1.
